title: Kigali International Airport

infobox.value: [Entebbe](/wiki/Entebbe_International_Airport "Entebbe International Airport"), [Grumeti](/wiki/Serengeti_National_Park "Serengeti National Park"), [Mwanza](/wiki/Mwanza_Airport "Mwanza Airport"), [Seronera](/wiki/Seronera_Airstrip "Seronera Airstrip")

([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **KGL**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **HRYR**), formerly known as Kanombe International Airport, is the primary [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") serving [Kigali](/wiki/Kigali "Kigali"), the capital of [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"). As of June 2024, there is an ongoing project to build another mega-airport in [Bugesera District](/wiki/Bugesera_District "Bugesera District"), [Eastern Province](/wiki/Eastern_Province,_Rwanda "Eastern Province, Rwanda"), which will be the biggest and the main air gateway for all destinations in the country, in addition to serving as a transit airport for [Goma](/wiki/Goma "Goma") and [Bukavu](/wiki/Bukavu "Bukavu") in the eastern [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There are 4 airlines based at Kigali Airport: [RwandAir](/wiki/RwandAir "RwandAir"), the flag carrier airline of Rwanda; [Akagera Aviation](/w/index.php?title=Akagera_Aviation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Akagera Aviation (page does not exist)"), a Rwandan helicopter airline;[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Tempus Jet](/w/index.php?title=Tempus_Jet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tempus Jet (page does not exist)"), an American airline providing charter flights;[[3]](#cite_note-3) and [Nexus Aero](/w/index.php?title=Nexus_Aero&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nexus Aero (page does not exist)"), a Saudi VIP airline.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

International airport in Rwanda

**Kigali International Airport** ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **KGL**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **HRYR**), formerly known as Kanombe International Airport, is the primary [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") serving [Kigali](/wiki/Kigali "Kigali"), the capital of [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"). As of June 2024, there is an ongoing project to build another mega-airport in [Bugesera District](/wiki/Bugesera_District "Bugesera District"), [Eastern Province](/wiki/Eastern_Province,_Rwanda "Eastern Province, Rwanda"), which will be the biggest and the main air gateway for all destinations in the country, in addition to serving as a transit airport for [Goma](/wiki/Goma "Goma") and [Bukavu](/wiki/Bukavu "Bukavu") in the eastern [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There are 4 airlines based at Kigali Airport: [RwandAir](/wiki/RwandAir "RwandAir"), the flag carrier airline of Rwanda; [Akagera Aviation](/w/index.php?title=Akagera_Aviation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Akagera Aviation (page does not exist)"), a Rwandan helicopter airline;[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Tempus Jet](/w/index.php?title=Tempus_Jet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tempus Jet (page does not exist)"), an American airline providing charter flights;[[3]](#cite_note-3) and [Nexus Aero](/w/index.php?title=Nexus_Aero&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nexus Aero (page does not exist)"), a Saudi VIP airline.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kigali_International_Airport&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Location")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Mil_Mi-17_Rwanda_Air_Force_-_Darfur_support%2C_U.S._Army_Africa%2C_Kigali%2C_Rwanda_090114.jpg/250px-Mil_Mi-17_Rwanda_Air_Force_-_Darfur_support%2C_U.S._Army_Africa%2C_Kigali%2C_Rwanda_090114.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mil_Mi-17_Rwanda_Air_Force_-_Darfur_support,_U.S._Army_Africa,_Kigali,_Rwanda_090114.jpg)

Mil Mi-17 Rwanda Air Force - Darfur support, U.S. Army Africa, Kigali, Rwanda 090114

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Rwandair_Express.jpg/250px-Rwandair_Express.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rwandair_Express.jpg)

Rwandair Express Aircraft at Kigali airport

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Kigali_airport_-_Flickr_-_Dave_Proffer.jpg/250px-Kigali_airport_-_Flickr_-_Dave_Proffer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kigali_airport_-_Flickr_-_Dave_Proffer.jpg)

Kigali International Airport

The airport is located in the suburb of *Kanombe Sector*, at the eastern edge of Kigali, approximately 5 kilometres (3.1 mi), by road, east of the central business district of the city of [Kigali](/wiki/Kigali "Kigali").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kigali_International_Airport&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

During the [Rwandan Civil War](/wiki/Rwandan_Civil_War "Rwandan Civil War"), Kigali airport was a major strategic point. Since Rwanda is a landlocked nation, this represented the only easy way in and out of the country. The airport had two runways, but after the [Arusha Accords](/wiki/Arusha_Accords_(Rwanda) "Arusha Accords (Rwanda)"), one runway was closed down after a request from the [Rwandan Patriotic Front](/wiki/Rwandan_Patriotic_Front "Rwandan Patriotic Front") (RPF). Later, in April 1994, [President Habyarimana's plane was shot down](/wiki/Assassination_of_Juv%C3%A9nal_Habyarimana_and_Cyprien_Ntaryamira "Assassination of Juvénal Habyarimana and Cyprien Ntaryamira"). It is disputed whether the RPF or the [Rwandan Defence Forces](/wiki/Rwandan_Defence_Forces "Rwandan Defence Forces") (FAR) shot down the plane. This event triggered a renewal of the civil war and the beginning of the [genocide against the Tutsi](/wiki/Rwandan_genocide "Rwandan genocide").

## Passenger traffic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kigali_International_Airport&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Passenger traffic")]

PassengersYear0200,000400,000600,000800,0001,000,0001,200,000200420072010201320162019PassengersAnnual passenger traffic

In 2008, the airport served 145,189 passengers, and about 290,000 passengers in 2010.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In May 2011, the Rwanda CAA announced that Kigali airport will be upgraded to meet the strong demand.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Works started in October 2012 and will be completed in May 2014.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 2012, data from Rwanda Civil Aviation Authority shows that passenger traffic through Kigali International Airport grew by 30 per cent to 488,903 last year, up from 377,327 in 2011.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The airport handled over 300 flights a week. The airport is designed to handle 400,000 passengers per year. International and domestic passenger numbers were nearly 600,000 in 2013,[[1]](#cite_note-youtube.com-1) while there were about 400 weekly flights.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] International and domestic passenger numbers totaled 710,000 in 2016.[[1]](#cite_note-youtube.com-1)

## Facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kigali_International_Airport&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Facilities")]

There are three terminals at Kigali. The main two-story terminal was built to replace the single-story building, now housing the VIP terminal. The main terminal can handle 6 small-to-midsized aircraft, but also up to a [Boeing 747](/wiki/Boeing_747 "Boeing 747") jet. The south side of the runway has two helicopter pads with access to the main runway, used by military helicopters. A cargo terminal is also located at the airport. The latest upgrades to tarmac and support systems were made in 2002. There is free [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") in the airport waiting area. In 2014, Kigali Airport ranked as the seventh-best regional airport in Africa, because of its capacity to respond to disaster, through its fire department (Category Nine), the second-best according to International Aviation Organisation standards.

[Rwandair](/wiki/Rwandair "Rwandair") has its head office on the top floor of the airport main building.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Newheadoffice-11) The airline previously had its head office in Centenary House in Kigali.[[12]](#cite_note-FI-12) The airline began moving its operations from Centenary House to the airport on Friday 14 May 2010. The airline was scheduled to be moved in by Monday 17 May 2010.[[11]](#cite_note-Newheadoffice-11)

In addition, Akagera Aviation, [Akagera Aviation School](/wiki/Akagera_Aviation_School "Akagera Aviation School"), and the [Rwanda Civil Aviation Authority](/wiki/Rwanda_Civil_Aviation_Authority "Rwanda Civil Aviation Authority") have their offices or are headquartered at the airport.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## New Kigali (Bugesera) International Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kigali_International_Airport&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New Kigali (Bugesera) International Airport")]

Main article: [Bugesera International Airport](/wiki/Bugesera_International_Airport "Bugesera International Airport")

There are plans to replace the current airport with a new one located south of Kigali on the south side of the Nyabarongo River in Bugesera. A new airport location is needed as the existing airport does not allow for upgrades such as additional runways and other facilities. The new airport will have one runway, with provisions for a second one to be added later. Construction activities for this airport started in 2017 and are planned to be completed in 2022.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In September 2016, a contract between the Rwandan government and [Mota-Engil Engenharia e Construção África, S.A.](/wiki/Mota-Engil "Mota-Engil"), a Portuguese firm, was signed. The company will construct the airport in two phases with works on the first phase now scheduled to begin in June 2017. After finishing the first phase by December 2018, Mota-Engil Engenharia e Construção África, S.A. will subsequently operate the airport for 25 years. The airport will have a capacity of 1.7 million passengers per year. A second phase that is planned to be built after that is supposed to raise the capacity of the airport to 4.5 million passengers per year. Mota-Engil Engenharia e Construção África, S.A. will cover the costs of US$418 million for the first phase and US$400 million for the second phase, leading to a total investment of US$818 million. As compensation, the company has the right to keep the profits from operating the airport. Under the agreement, the Rwandan government won't have to contribute to the costs of construction and operation.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In March 2019, the government confirmed that some works were temporarily put on hold in order for a redesign to take place.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The redesign will ensure that the airport is up to international standards.

In December 2019, [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar")'s state-owned airline, [Qatar Airways](/wiki/Qatar_Airways "Qatar Airways"), collaborated with the [Rwanda Development Board](/wiki/Rwanda_Development_Board "Rwanda Development Board") to purchase a 60% stake in the [Bugesera International Airport](/wiki/Bugesera_International_Airport "Bugesera International Airport"). Infrastructure minister [Claver Gatete](/wiki/Claver_Gatete "Claver Gatete") said, “We are looking for a bigger sized airport. That's why we are looking for a bigger investor.”[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Incidents and accidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kigali_International_Airport&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Incidents and accidents")]

* 6 April 1994 – A [Falcon 50](/wiki/Falcon_50 "Falcon 50") owned by and carrying then-president of Rwanda [Juvenal Habyarimana](/wiki/Juvenal_Habyarimana "Juvenal Habyarimana") was [shot down](/wiki/Assassination_of_Habyarimana_and_Ntaryamira "Assassination of Habyarimana and Ntaryamira") while approaching the airport, killing all 12 aboard including Habyarimana and then-president of [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi"), [Cyprien Ntaryamira](/wiki/Cyprien_Ntaryamira "Cyprien Ntaryamira"), who were returning from a meeting to end the Rwandan civil war. The wreckage landed in front of the presidential palace. The attack was blamed on Tutsi rebels, and, as a result, within one hour of the crash [Interahamwe](/wiki/Interahamwe "Interahamwe") militias began the [Rwandan genocide](/wiki/Rwandan_genocide "Rwandan genocide"). There is no consensus on who actually shot down or ordered the attack on the plane.
* 1 June 2004 – An [Antonov 32](/wiki/Antonov_32 "Antonov 32") owned by Sun Air (9XR-SN), reportedly suffered some problems with the left main undercarriage after takeoff from [Beni, Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Beni,_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Beni, Democratic Republic of the Congo"). The airplane was headed for [Goma](/wiki/Goma "Goma"), but diverted to Kigali for an emergency landing. The aircraft crashed on landing, causing the Russian pilots and Congolese passengers to sustain serious injuries. The An-32 involved in the accident had been detained in Goma mid-July 2003 because it carried a shipment of armaments destined for a Rwanda-backed militia in the Congolese Kasai region.
* 12 November 2009, [RwandAir Flight 205](/wiki/RwandAir_Flight_205 "RwandAir Flight 205"), a [Bombardier CRJ-100](/wiki/Bombardier_CRJ-100 "Bombardier CRJ-100") crashed into a VIP terminal shortly after an emergency landing; out of the 10 passengers and 5 crew, 1 passenger died.
